exemplar.title: To what extent does the simple frequency of the standing wave (Hz) affect the corrected air column height in a resonance tube experiment to determine the speed of sound?"

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does the simple frequency of the standing wave (Hz) affect the corrected air column height in a resonance tube experiment to determine the speed of sound?"

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data recording and processing are communicated with clarity and precision, and the graph with error bars greatly enhances interpretability.
Uncertainties for column height are correctly propagated using random spread, and significant figures are handled appropriately.
Raw and processed tables, despite minor layout issues, present the necessary data for independent review.
weaknesses: Consideration of uncertainties is incomplete: frequency and fit-parameter uncertainties are not propagated, and percentage uncertainties are inconsistently reported.
There are internal inconsistencies between the two calculated speeds of sound (328.52 m/s vs 349.69 m/s).
Unit conversions in the slope-to-speed calculation lack explicit clarity, with unexplained division factors.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates strong clarity and precision in data presentation, meriting a high score of 5. Minor omissions in uncertainty propagation and processing inaccuracies prevent a full topâband mark, but the overall analysis quality is robust.

strengths: The research question is situated within a highly specific and appropriate context, demonstrating deep understanding of resonance tube acoustics.
The apparatus list, variable table, photographs, and narrative method allow for full reproducibility of the dataâcollection procedure.
Background theory on standing waves, end correction, and temperature influence is clearly articulated and directly linked to the experimental aims.
weaknesses: The exact immersion depth of the outer tube and the precise positioning of the tuning fork are not specified, risking minor ambiguities in replication.
Literature citations for temperature dependence and quantitative justification of experimental parameters are omitted.
Explanations of why certain controls (e.g., choice of three trials, end-correction usage) matter remain somewhat generic rather than fully explicative.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although one methodological strand fell slightly short of a full explanation, the studentâs clear contextual framing, comprehensive procedure description, and strong theoretical linkage justify a topâband holistic score of 6. The minor omissions do not undermine the overall strength and reproducibility of the design.

strengths: The conclusion is directly justified by the analysis, with a clear reasoning chain from frequency through height to the speed of sound.
A quantitative comparison of the measured speed to the textbook value is provided, situating the result in scientific context.
The inverse relationship between frequency and corrected column height is restated clearly and consistently.
weaknesses: Numerical inconsistencies in reported speed values persist, which undermines full confidence in the conclusion.
Uncertainty analysis is not fully integrated into the final statement of results.
Further literature comparison (e.g., temperatureâdependent variations) is lacking, limiting the depth of scientific context.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is well linked to the data and scientific context, justifying a strong score of 5. Only minor inconsistencies and limited integration of uncertainties prevent the absolute top band.

strengths: The student identifies specific methodological weaknesses (temperature fluctuation, ruler precision, limited trials, fork loudness) rather than generic limitations.
Suggested improvements (temperature control, digital tools, increased trial number) are realistic and directly relevant to the investigation.
The evaluation table is organized and clearly presents both weaknesses and possible remedies.
weaknesses: The relative impact of each methodological weakness on the final speed value is not analyzed.
Improvements are described but not explained quantitatively in terms of how they would reduce uncertainties or alter results.
There is limited depth in discussing how suggested changes would improve accuracy or precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Specific weaknesses are described and relevant improvements are proposed, meriting a score of 4. The lack of quantitative impact analysis or explanation of how each improvement would reduce uncertainty prevents a higher mark.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student formulates a clear research question and embeds it in the very specific context of measuring the speed of sound with a resonance tube at room temperature.  Background theory on resonance tubes, end correction and temperature influence is discussed before the question appears, showing the reader exactly how the investigation sits within standard acoustic physics.  While the context does not go as far as citing quantitative literature values for temperature dependence, the framing is sufficiently narrow and appropriate to merit the upper band.

Instrument uncertainties are quoted for ruler and thermometer, random spread is used to find âh and âv, and error bars appear on the graph.  Nevertheless, frequency uncertainty is ignored, propagation through the linear-fit method is incomplete and the final percentage uncertainty is inconsistently reported (4.39 % vs 2.46 %).  These omissions are âsignificantâ but some consideration is evident, so the strand falls in the middle band with a score of 3.

Methodological considerations (controls, errors, choice of three trials, end correction, temperature) are described and linked to data relevance.  However, explanations of WHY each control matters are brief and somewhat generic; for instance the influence of temperature is noted but not quantified, and sufficiency of data points (only eight frequencies) is not discussed.  The strand therefore fits a competent description rather than a full explanation.

Specific weaknesses (temperature fluctuation, ruler precision, limited trials, fork loudness) are listed in a table, demonstrating that the student has moved beyond generic statements.  However, the relative impact on the final speed value is not analysed; the weaknesses are merely âdescribedâ.  This aligns with the middle band, lower score.

The apparatus list, the table of variables, photographs and a narrative method together allow another investigator to reproduce the data-collection sequence without major doubt: fork frequencies, three trials each, ruler precision, tube diameter and end-correction factor are all stated.  Minor ambiguities (exact immersion depth of the outer cylinder, precise positioning of the fork) remain but would not prevent replication, justifying placement in the top band with a conservative score of 5 rather than 6.

All essential calculations (end correction, averages, wavelength, speed, slope) are shown, but unit handling is shaky (division by 100 when converting slope) and there is an internal inconsistency between the two speed-of-sound values quoted (328 m sâ»Â¹ vs 350 m sâ»Â¹).  These inaccuracies limit confidence, so processing cannot be judged âaccurateâ, placing the work low in the 3-4 band.

Improvements such as temperature control, digital measuring tools and increased trials are realistic and relevant.  Yet the student does not explain quantitatively how each suggestion would reduce the stated uncertainties or alter the conclusion, hence they are âdescribedâ rather than âexplainedâ.  A score of 3 in the 3-4 band is therefore appropriate.

Raw and processed data tables are present and units are usually supplied, and a graph with error bars is included.  Column alignment in the raw table is untidy and some headings are unclear, but overall the recording is readable (clear) and numerical values carry the correct significant figures (precise).  These qualities match the 3-4 band; the stronger end of the band is awarded because the graph aids clarity substantially.

The student briefly compares the measured 346 m sâ»Â¹ to the textbook value 346 m sâ»Â¹ and notes the small deviation, but no temperature correction or further literature comparison is offered.  This constitutes âsome relevant comparisonâ rather than a justified one, meriting a score of 3 in the 3-4 band.

The conclusion restates the inverse relationship and provides a final speed with uncertainty, directly linked to the processed data and graph.  While numerical inconsistencies remain, the reasoning chain frequency â height â speed is explicit and largely consistent.  Hence the conclusion is âdescribedâ and mostly supported, fitting the 3-4 band, with the higher score awarded for the clear qualitative linkage.

annotations.comment: The propagation of uncertainties for column height (Îh) is correctly computed using spread; this enhances the reliability of the error bars.

The introduction provides a clear overview of standing wave resonance but omits citations of literature values for temperature dependence, which would strengthen contextual framing and justify the chosen experimental parameters.
The student effectively explains the significance of standing wave resonance in practical acoustics and technology, linking theory to realâworld applications.
The derivation of the standing wave equation $$y=(2A\,\sin kx)\cos\omega t$$ is presented clearly and accurately, demonstrating solid understanding of wave superposition.
After deriving the standing wave form, the student could more directly connect node positions to the resonance tube experiment, explicitly bridging theory and method.
The conditions for nodes (\(kx=0,\pi,2\pi,...\)) are accurately derived, showing correct application of boundary conditions.
The explanation of nodes would benefit from a simple schematic to accompany the text, improving clarity for readers less familiar with standing-wave patterns.
Discussion of antinode positions and normal modes enriches the background and situates the experiment within broader wave mechanics.
The text on boundary conditions could explicitly reference the closed-open tube configuration used in the experiment to maintain consistency in theoretical framing.
The description of higher harmonics and harmonic series in strings is technically correct, though sentence structure in the inline math section could be tightened for readability.
The student gives a clear and concise definition of resonance in vibrating systems, emphasizing its importance in musical acoustics.
The figures lack captions or descriptive labels. Adding concise figure captions would improve reproducibility by clarifying what each image depicts.
The discussion of displacement nodes and pressure antinodes at open and closed ends is thorough, demonstrating a good grasp of boundary conditions in air columns.
The research question is specific, well-phrased, and directly linked to the experimentâs aims, providing a solid foundation for the investigation.
Consider defining âcorrected air column heightâ operationally here, previewing the end-correction calculation to guide readers.
The conceptual explanation of resonance in standing waves is articulated clearly, linking nodes and antinodes to measurement significance.
The method description omits specifics about how deep the outer tube is immersed and how the tuning fork is positioned; include these to ensure reproducibility.
When presenting \(\lambda=4L\), mention the end-correction factor here to connect theoretical formulae directly to corrected measurements.
The variable list on the schematic page includes only the independent variable; adding the dependent and controlled variables here would complete the design description.
The rationale for choosing the resonance tube method is thorough and convincingly linked to experimental goals, highlighting simplicity and precision.
Quantifying the expected instrument uncertainties during method justification (e.g., ruler Â±0.1 cm) would better prepare readers for later uncertainty analysis.
The raw data table header is misaligned and omits units for each trial column; reorganize headings and include units clearly for readability.
Unit conversion in the processed data table shows division by 100 in slope-to-speed conversion; clarify this operation or correct it to avoid confusion.
When converting the average height from cm to m, explicitly state the operation (e.g., \(32.62\,\mathrm{cm}=0.3262\,\mathrm{m}\)) to maintain unit consistency.
The calculated mean speed of 328.52 m/s later conflicts with the slope-derived value of 349.69 m/s; ensure consistency between different calculation methods.
The graph of inverse frequency vs. height with error bars substantially improves clarity of the linear relationship.
To fully characterize the fit, calculate and report uncertainties in slope and intercept, not only RÂ², to demonstrate precision.
The interpretation correctly identifies RÂ²â1 as a strong fit, showing the studentâs understanding of linear regression metrics.
The trendline equations are missing explicit '+' signs between slope and intercept; reformat to standard \(y=mx+b\) notation for clarity.
The conclusion restates the inverse relationship but does not integrate uncertainty analysis or compare the result quantitatively to the literature value.
The student situates the measured speed (346.4Â±15.4 m/s) against a textbook value, providing valuable scientific context.
The table lists realistic improvements (e.g., temperature-controlled room) but does not explain quantitatively how each will reduce measured uncertainties.
The critical evaluation mentions controlling air temperature but lacks analysis of its relative impact on final speed, limiting depth of evaluation.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

similarExemplars.title:
How does the length of the pipe affect the natural frequency of the air column in the pipe at constant air temperature when the pipe is:
a)	open at both ends and air is blown close to one end?
b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.

userSubmission.researchQuestion:
How does the length of the pipe affect the natural frequency of the air column in the pipe at constant air temperature when the pipe is:
a)	open at both ends and air is blown close to one end?
b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.
